data.title: Corn Futures Fall After Trump Halts Trade Negotiations With Canada -- Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais


-- Corn for December delivery fell 0.9% to $4.24 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Friday, under pressure throughout the day after President Trump said that trade negotiations between the U.S. and major agricultural buyer Canada were terminated.

-- Soybeans for November delivery fell 0.3% to $10.42 1/4 a bushel.

-- Wheat for December delivery were unchanged at $5.13 a bushel.


HIGHLIGHTS


Cutting Ties: CBOT grain futures were under pressure throughout the day in reaction to President Trump's declaration that trade talks with Canada have been terminated, this because of an anti-tariff ad using clips of Ronald Reagan and run by the Canadian government.

Harvest pressure is also contributing to lower futures, with U.S. farmers nearing completion of their harvesting and more U.S. grains enter the pipeline, said AgMarket.net in a note.


Outside of the Box: Without fresh USDA data because of the ongoing government shutdown, traders are taking their indicators from other reports such as the International Grains Council's monthly report. The IGC forecasts record-high global wheat production at 827 million metric tons, as well as higher global wheat stocks.

"The data thus provided a posteriori explanation for the decline in wheat prices to their lowest level in five years," said Commerzbank in a note.

Wheat floated between either side of unchanged throughout the day, before closing flat.


INSIGHT


Change of Pace: Corn futures led row crop futures lower. But some analysts see strong demand for corn, coming from the U.S. ethanol industry and a strong export market, supporting a bigger turnaround in prices.

"I am friendly to corn," said Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing in a note. "Yields are not there. Exports are on fire. Domestic demand [is] strong."

With the government shutdown, the USDA hasn't issued any updates to harvesting and crop conditions in weeks.


Band-Aid: The partial reopening of the USDA's Farm Service Agency is allowing farmers access to critical financial support, but the U.S. agricultural community needs the government to fully reopen in order for farmers to better prepare for the next planting season, the National Young Farmers Coalition said.

"Farmers face a dangerous combination of uncertainty, instability and loss of support from the very agencies meant to keep this country running smoothly," the group says in a press release. The group adds that "this limited restart is not a real solution."

Farm income in 2025 has been impacted by both low commodity prices and high input costs.


AHEAD


Note: The government shutdown is impacting the timing of USDA reports and data releases.


-- The USDA is scheduled to release its weekly grain export inspections report at 11 a.m. EDT Monday.

-- The USDA is due to release its weekly crop progress report at 4 p.m. EDT Monday.

-- Smithfield Foods Inc. is scheduled to release its third-quarter earnings report before the stock market opens on Tuesday.

-- Mondelez International Inc. is due to release its third-quarter earnings report at 4:05 p.m. EDT Tuesday.


Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-24-25 1602ET

data.title: Wall Street notches record closing highs on cool inflation, solid earnings
Sector Update: Financial Stocks Higher Late Afternoon
Sector Update: Tech Stocks Rise Late Afternoon
US Equity Indexes Rise as Inflation Cools
Signs of Stress in Consumers, Says Metlife's Drew Matus
data.description:
US consumer sentiment fell in October to a five-month low, as worries persisted about stubbornly high prices and the impact on their finances. The final October sentiment index fell to 53.6 from 55.1 in September, a deterioration from the preliminary reading, according to the University of Michigan. A measure of current conditions dropped to the lowest since August 2022. MetLife Investment Management Chief Market Strategist Drew Matus believes that the soft CPI report is hinting towards a K-shaped economy, causing signs of stress among upper end consumers. According to Matus these consumers have a high level of job separation anxiety leading to a pull back on personal expenses, saying 'if you're not going to buy a cup of coffee you're not going to buy a car." He joined Carol Massar and Tim Stenovec on 'Bloomberg Businessweek Daily' to break it down.

Bloomberg videos, provided by MT Newswires

data.title: ICE Review : Canola Enters Weekend in the Red
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange erased early gains before closing lower on Friday to go with negative sentiment in vegetable oils.

An analyst said the January canola contract will need to reach C$640 per tonne to gain "meaningful bullishness". He also said the planted area for canola in 2026 will be "interesting" due to the current trade tensions and canola's price structure.

Chicago soyoil, European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil were down while crude oil was steady to lower.

At mid-afternoon, the Canadian dollar declined less than one-tenth of a U.S. cent compared to Thursday's close. U.S. President Donald Trump cancelled all trade talks with Canada on Thursday after the Ontario government aired a TV ad featuring a 1987 address by then-president Ronald Reagan speaking against tariffs.

There were 53,260 canola contracts traded on Friday, compared to Thursday when 55,776 contracts changed hands. Spreading accounted for 34,878 of the contracts traded.

Settlement prices are in Canadian dollars per metric tonne.
